description: The provided image is entirely unreadable for scientific analysis, displaying only a generic Check for updates icon with no visible axes, curves, micrographs, legends, or sub-panels. Therefore, no direct visual observations of precipitate morphology, diffraction peaks, or mechanical response can be extracted from the pixel data. The accompanying context identifies the study as investigating Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels (M1 without Ti/V and M2 with Ti/V microalloying) subjected to solution treatment at 1000 °C and aging at 550 °C. Reported characterization includes OM, SEM, TEM, EBSD, and tensile testing, with documented figures encompassing engineering stress-strain curves, true stress-strain and strain-hardening rate plots, Crussard-Jaoul work-hardening index analyses, strengthening mechanism contribution charts, and microstructural evolution schematics. However, because the image itself lacks any discernible scientific content and the caption field is empty, the specific figure identity and its constituent data panels cannot be determined from the available evidence.

This figure presents a comparative set of optical micrographs showing the metallographic structure of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels across four conditions: (a) M1-SS, (b) M1-30, (c) M2-SS, and (d) M2-30, with a scale bar of 50 μm applying to all images. The micrographs reveal equiaxed austenite grain morphologies in all samples. Visually, the M1 series ((a) and (b)) exhibits coarser grain structures compared to the Ti/V-modified M2 series ((c) and (d)), indicating a grain-refinement effect from microalloying. The caption identifies (a) as solution-treated M1, (b) as M1 aged at 550°C for 30 h, (c) as solution-treated M2, and (d) as M2 aged at 550°C for 30 h. According to the text, M1-SS shows a single-phase austenite matrix with an average grain size of 42.4 ± 7.8 μm, while M1-30 retains a similar grain size (~45.3 μm) but contains uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbide precipitates not individually resolvable by optical microscopy. The M2 steel exhibits significantly finer grains (~32.3 μm in M2-SS and ~35.8 μm in M2-30) due to intragranular (Ti,V)C carbides; after aging, κ-carbides heterogeneously precipitate at the edges of these (Ti,V)C particles. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that Ti/V addition refines the austenite grain structure and alters the κ-carbide precipitation mode from uniform intragranular distribution to heterogeneous nucleation on pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides.

Figure 2 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation maps comparing the grain structure of alloy M2 (a Ti/V-modified Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel) in the solution-treated state (a, M2-SS) and after aging at 550 °C for 30 h (b, M2-30). Both maps display equiaxed austenite grains with a seemingly random color distribution across the orientation triangle—visually consistent with the absence of strong crystallographic texture—and share an identical 50 μm scale bar; panel (b) includes the standard IPF color key for the [001]–[101]–[111] crystallographic directions. While the images directly confirm a recrystallized, equiaxed matrix in both conditions, related EBSD analysis reported in the text indicates that M2-SS exhibits a bimodal grain-size distribution, whereas M2-30 shows a unimodal distribution with modest grain coarsening during aging. These orientation maps provide essential microstructural context, demonstrating that Ti/V microalloying produces a fine-grained, randomly textured austenite matrix and that aging does not induce preferential grain orientation, thereby supporting the study’s central argument that precipitation of (Ti,V)C carbides and heterogeneous κ-carbides dictates the mechanical property evolution.

Figure 3 presents bright-field (BF) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images comparing the microstructures of as-solution-treated M1-SS (a) and M2-SS (b) in an Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel. Subfigure (a) shows a grain boundary region in the Ti/V-free M1-SS sample, revealing a clean austenite matrix devoid of visible precipitates. Subfigure (b) displays the Ti/V-modified M2-SS sample, where intragranular nanoscale (Ti,V)C carbides are visible and explicitly indicated by blue arrows and labels within the austenite grains. This direct comparison demonstrates that Ti/V microalloying produces stable (Ti,V)C precipitates that remain in the matrix after solution treatment, contrasting sharply with the precipitate-free M1-SS condition and providing the heterogeneous nucleation sites responsible for localized κ-carbide formation during subsequent aging.

This figure presents comprehensive TEM characterization of precipitates in Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels, comparing Ti/V-free (M1) and Ti/V-modified (M2) alloys after aging at 550°C. Panels (a–c) show BF, DF, and SAED (ZA=[011]) images of sample M1-30, revealing a high density of uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbides throughout the austenite matrix. Panels (d–f) for M2-10 display BF, DF, and SAED (ZA=[1̄10]) images demonstrating the coexistence of κ-carbides and pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides, where κ-carbides preferentially precipitate heterogeneously at the edges of (Ti,V)C particles with almost no matrix precipitation. Panels (g–j) for M2-30 present BF, CDF images of both phases, and SAED (ZA=[100]), confirming that after 30 h aging the κ-carbides exhibit increased number density and larger sizes but remain heterogeneously distributed at (Ti,V)C edges. The accompanying table reports quantitative precipitate data: M1-30 contains only κ-carbides (volume fraction 7.4±3.4%, mean radius 10.8±6.2 nm), whereas M2-30 contains both (Ti,V)C (2.3±1.7%, 37.1±22.6 nm) and κ-carbides (3.2±2.8%, 9.7±8.5 nm). These results establish that (Ti,V)C carbides fundamentally alter κ-carbide precipitation behavior by providing heterogeneous nucleation sites, shifting the distribution from uniform intragranular to localized interphase precipitation.

This figure presents a bright-field (BF) TEM image and the corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping for sample M2-30, a Ti/V-modified Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h. The BF micrograph identifies a (Ti,V)C carbide particle and an adjacent κ-carbide precipitate at its edge. The eight elemental distribution maps for Fe, Mn, Al, Si, Cr, Mo, V, and Ti reveal strong V and Ti enrichment at the (Ti,V)C carbide, while Fe represents the austenite matrix. Mn, Al, Si, Cr, and Mo show relatively uniform distributions without pronounced segregation to the precipitates. All panels share a 50 nm scale bar. These observations provide direct microchemical evidence supporting the conclusion that pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides act as heterogeneous nucleation sites for κ-carbide precipitation, thereby modifying the precipitation morphology compared to the uniform intragranular κ-carbide distribution in Ti/V-free variants.

Figure 6 presents a multi-scale transmission electron microscopy investigation of precipitates in sample M2-30, a Ti/V-modified Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h. Panel (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM image showing intragranular (Ti, V)C carbides and adjacent κ-carbides, with a scale bar of 50 nm. Panel (b) provides a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the matrix and precipitates at atomic resolution (scale bar 10 nm), revealing the intergrowth structure at the interface between the (Ti, V)C carbide and the κ-carbide. Panel (c) displays the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) diffraction pattern along the [110] zone axis, identifying reflections from the γ-austenite matrix (002), the (Ti, V)C carbide (002), and the κ-carbide (001). The FFT pattern confirms the crystallographic orientation relationship between (Ti, V)C and κ-carbide, specifically that the (002) plane of (Ti, V)C is parallel to the (001) plane of κ-carbide, supporting heterogeneous nucleation of κ-carbides at the edges of pre-existing (Ti, V)C carbides.

Figure 7 presents two mechanical-property subplots for Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress versus engineering strain curves for four conditions identified by the legend as M1-SS, M1-30, M2-SS, and M2-30. The M2-30 specimen exhibits the highest flow stress and ultimate tensile strength, while M1-SS displays the largest uniform elongation exceeding an engineering strain of 0.5. Subplot (b) overlays true stress–true strain curves (left y-axis) with strain hardening rate curves (right y-axis) for the same four samples. The M2-30 alloy begins with the highest strain hardening rate, which then declines steeply, whereas M1-SS maintains a lower but more persistent hardening rate to large true strains. These curves collectively demonstrate that the Ti/V-modified and aged M2-30 condition achieves greater strength than M1-30 while preserving superior work-hardening capacity, supporting the conclusion that heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation associated with pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides enhances the strength–ductility balance.

Figure 8 presents EPMA images of the solution-treated Ti/V-modified steel M2-SS. Subfigure (a) shows the mapping domain with a dashed outline marking the (Ti, V)C carbide particle, while subfigure (b) displays the corresponding carbon distribution map around this precipitate. The color scale in (b) indicates X-ray counts from 6.00 to 136.00, with intense red/yellow at the center confirming severe carbon enrichment within the (Ti, V)C carbide. Toward the edges, the carbon signal gradually decreases, yet a surrounding carbon-rich region exceeding the matrix level is clearly visible. Both images share a 100 nm scale bar, placing the carbide in the nanometer range. These observations directly demonstrate that (Ti, V)C carbides act as carbon sinks, creating a local carbon gradient. This supports the paper’s conclusion that pre-aging formation of (Ti, V)C effectively modulates residual matrix carbon, thereby governing the subsequent heterogeneous nucleation of κ-carbides at the carbide edges during aging.

Figure 9 presents a schematic diagram illustrating the precipitation behavior of κ-carbides and (Ti, V)C carbides in the M2 Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel during aging at 550 °C. The left panel depicts an austenite grain containing pre-existing intragranular (Ti, V)C carbides, where the color gradient indicates that carbon content is highest at the carbide center and decreases toward the edges. The right panel shows the microstructure after aging at 550 °C, where κ-carbides heterogeneously precipitate preferentially along the edges of the (Ti, V)C carbides rather than uniformly throughout the matrix. The legend identifies the blue hexagon as the austenite grain, white circles as (Ti, V)C carbides, and gray rectangles as κ-carbides. This schematic conveys that the carbon-enriched zones surrounding (Ti, V)C carbides provide favorable heterogeneous nucleation sites for κ-carbides, which is central to the Ti/V microalloying strategy for controlling precipitation morphology and thereby balancing strength and work-hardening capacity.

This figure presents a stacked bar chart (Fig. 10) quantifying the contributions of distinct strengthening mechanisms—solid-solution strengthening (σ₀), grain-boundary strengthening (σ_G), Orowan bypass strengthening (σ_Or), and shear strengthening from κ-carbides (σ_S)—to the yield strength of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels, specifically the Ti/V-free M1 and Ti/V-modified M2 steels. Four conditions are compared: M1-SS and M2-SS in the solution-treated state, and M1-30 and M2-30 after aging at 550 °C for 30 h. The measured yield strengths (red bars) are approximately 430 MPa for M1-SS, 860 MPa for M1-30, ~660 MPa for M2-SS, and ~880 MPa for M2-30. In M1-SS and M1-30, strength derives solely from σ₀, σ_G, and σ_S, with the large σ_S contribution (~430 MPa) in M1-30 driving the strength increase upon aging. In M2-SS, Orowan strengthening from intragranular (Ti,V)C carbides (σ_Or ≈ 120 MPa) supplements σ₀ and σ_G, raising the baseline strength above M1-SS. After aging, M2-30 combines σ_Or from (Ti,V)C with a reduced but significant σ_S (~220 MPa) from κ-carbides precipitated heterogeneously at (Ti,V)C edges, achieving a total yield strength comparable to M1-30 while maintaining a distinct precipitation architecture. The data support the conclusion that Ti/V microalloying replaces uniform κ-carbide shear strengthening with (Ti,V)C bypass strengthening plus heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation, sustaining high yield strength.

The figure presents Crussard-Jaoul plots of work hardening index, ln(dσ/dε) versus lnε, for Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels. Panel (a) shows M1 (Ti/V-free): M1-SS (solid-solution, gray squares) exhibits three stages (I, II, III) with a rapid Stage-I drop followed by a plateau and a late upturn, whereas M1-30 (aged at 550°C for 30 h, black triangles) shows only two stages with a suppressed plateau. Panel (b) shows M2 (Ti/V-modified): M2-SS (pink squares) and M2-30 (red triangles) curves nearly overlap, both displaying two-stage behavior. Linear fits are overlaid on the data points. The comparison indicates that uniform intragranular κ-carbide precipitation in M1 reduces work-hardening capacity and eliminates Stage III after aging, while Ti/V microalloying in M2 preserves work-hardening ability because κ-carbides heterogeneously precipitate at (Ti,V)C carbide edges, leaving the matrix less affected.

The figure comprises three transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs showing dislocation structures in tensile-deformed Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels. Subfigure (a) depicts the aged M1-30 specimen (200 nm scale bar), revealing planar slip bands marked by yellow dashed lines with comparatively wide inter-band spacing. Subfigure (b) presents the aged M2-30 specimen (200 nm scale bar), where the marked slip bands are noticeably narrower and more densely spaced, indicating more severe plastic deformation; the blue square denotes the region magnified in subfigure (c). Subfigure (c) (30 nm scale bar) resolves intragranular (Ti, V)C carbides dispersed in the matrix. The accompanying text reports that these non-shearable carbides impede dislocation motion and generate a locally higher dislocation density around them, whereas the homogeneous κ-carbides in M1-30 are shearable and soften glide planes. Consequently, the microalloying addition in M2-30 refines the slip-band spacing and helps sustain a higher work-hardening capacity relative to M1-30.
